exemplar.title: To what extent will Starbucks changing their payment process to cashless lead to a positive impact on their sales revenue?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent will Starbucks changing their payment process to cashless lead to a positive impact on their sales revenue?

subject.title: Business Management
subject.description:
**IB Business Management** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **business fundamentals, human resource management, finance and accounts, marketing, and operations management**, with a strong focus on **case study analysis, real-world business applications, and strategic decision-making**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| **Assessment** | **Format** | **SL** | **HL** | **Weighting** |
|----------------|------------|--------|--------|---------------|
| **Paper 1** | Case study-based exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 25% |
| **Paper 2** | Core themes exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | Social enterprise case study | N/A | 1h 15min | HL: 25% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Written commentary (SL) / Research project (HL) | Various | Various | SL: 30%, HL: 20% |

strengths: Uses Starbucksâ UK revenue, transaction volumes and demographics to anchor the discussion
Explicitly defines âchangeâ as the key concept and revisits it when applying Lewinâs Force-Field Analysis
weaknesses: Occasional claims (e.g., national debit-card ownership) remain loosely tied to Starbucks
Conceptual references are not woven into every numerical calculation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments place performance firmly in band 4: there is a strong, clear analytical grasp of âchangeâ linked to organisational data throughout, but the integration is not fully comprehensive in every section, so 4/5 accurately reflects the quality.

strengths: Provides exactly five relevant appendices (e.g., cost-savings table, financials)
Documents are sufficiently in-depth to support the main arguments (e.g., Barclays fees justify cost analysis)
weaknesses: Several sources (blogs, popular media) lack scholarly robustness
No critical evaluation of source reliability or methodology
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Supporting documents meet band 3 descriptors: the quantity (3â5) and general relevance/depth are solid, but they fall short of the breadth, critical appraisal and depth required for band 4.

strengths: Lewinâs Force-Field Analysis is applied directly to the research question
Segmentation table effectively illustrates demographic and psychographic criteria
weaknesses: Only one formal analytical model is used; lacks additional tools (e.g., SWOT, costâbenefit)
Some applications are superficial (e.g., unexamined assumptions about time savings)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Tool selection is limited (band 1) and application is adequate (band 3), sitting overall at the lower mid-band. A score of 2/4 reflects the need for a broader toolkit and deeper critical application.
feedback.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

strengths: Numeric data (transaction times, revenues, demographic percentages) is used in calculations
Provides balanced evaluation (e.g., exclusion risks vs. savings) and step-by-step computations
Force-field analysis distinguishes driving and resisting forces clearly
weaknesses: Key assumptions (500 daily transactions, 90% cashless rate) are unvalidated
Logical leaps in converting saved seconds to sales; no sensitivity analysis
Some data lack sourcing or methodological transparency
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work exhibits band 3 performance: selection and use of data are adequate, and there is meaningful analysis, but depth and integration are uneven. A score of 3/5 accurately captures this level.

strengths: Conclusions fully consistent with evidence (e.g., revenue and cost savings figures)
Explicitly answers the research question, recommending cashless adoption with caveats
weaknesses: Does not propose implementation details (e.g., phased roll-out or pilot testing)
Lacks specific change-management strategies to mitigate identified risks
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Both strands sit at band 3: conclusions align with evidence and directly address the question. The lack of detailed implementation planning prevents reaching band 4, so 3/3 is justified.

strengths: Logical organization and clear headings throughout the report
Includes title page, contents page, paginated sections and appendices in a coherent sequence
weaknesses: Table of contents lacks page numbers for each heading
Word-count compliance is noted but could be flagged if out of range
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The structure is appropriate and consistent with band 2 descriptors, showing clear organization throughout. Thus, 2/2 is warranted.

strengths: All required IB presentation elements are present and clearly labelled
Title page, bibliography and appendices adhere to IB guidelines
weaknesses: Equations could be formatted more clearly (e.g., LaTeX notation)
Minor formatting improvements (e.g., consistent font/spacing) would enhance readability
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation fully meets the highest band: all required elements are included and clearly presented. A score of 2/2 reflects this compliance.

strandSelections.reasoning: The report follows a logical sequence (title, contents, intro, analysis with sub-headings, conclusion, bibliography, appendices).  Headings and pagination make navigation easy, satisfying the âappropriate structureâ descriptor.

Most documents are clearly linked to points made (e.g., Barclays fees support cost-saving calculation; Timetric data supports speed claim).  Sources are generally relevant, yet depth is mixedâseveral come from blogs or popular media rather than robust industry reports, and critical evaluation of reliability is absent.  This aligns with ârelevant and sufficiently in-depthâ rather than the richer âin-depth and range of ideasâ required for band 4.
All IB-required elementsâtitle page, contents, word count, bibliography, appendicesâare present and clearly labelled, fulfilling the highest presentation band.

Exactly five appendices are supplied (payment-speed article, Barclays tariff table, Starbucks financials, segmentation table, technology-risk article).  This sits within the required 3-5 range and therefore matches a band that awards full credit for quantity.

The Lewin diagram and cost/revenue calculations are applied directly to the research question, and links to Starbucksâ situation are generally clear.  Nonetheless, some applications are surface-level (e.g., assumes all time saved converts to extra sales) and relevance is not always critically examined, matching âadequately appliedâ rather than âclear and effectiveâ in band 4.

A range of numeric data (transaction times, store counts, revenue, demographic percentages) is selected and used in calculations.  While some figures are assumptive or lack sourcing detail, overall selection/use is adequate and supports discussion, fitting band 3.

The analysis moves beyond descriptionâquantifying projected revenue gains and savings, and weighing them against exclusion risks and system outagesâyet depth of evaluation is uneven and some logical leaps remain.  This constitutes âsome analysisâ of the question rather than the mostly effective, integrated analysis of band 4.
The student explicitly answers the research questionârecommending Starbucks go cashless while acknowledging conditionsâthus meeting the descriptor for an explicit answer.

Only one recognised analytical toolâthe Lewin Force Field Analysisâis employed, augmented by simple arithmetic revenue projections.  This reflects a limited selection rather than the broader toolkit expected in higher bands.

The conclusion reiterates numerical findings and mirrors evidence presented (e.g., revenue gain of Â£33m, savings of Â£34m, risks of exclusion/technical failure), displaying full consistency with cited data.

Starbucks is at the centre of every section: revenue figures, transaction volumes, customer demographics and store numbers are repeatedly used to anchor arguments.  The analysis clearly links organisational data (UK revenue, branch count, demographic profile) to the proposed change.  However, a few general claims (e.g., national debit-card ownership) are only loosely tied back to Starbucks, so the integration is strong but not âcomprehensive throughoutâ, placing it in band 4.

The student explicitly identifies âchangeâ as the key concept, defines it, and revisits it when applying Lewinâs Force-Field Analysis and when discussing drivers and resistors throughout the advantages/disadvantages section.  References to âthe changeâ are integrated at several decision points, showing strong conceptual grasp, yet the discussion is not woven consistently through every argument (e.g., some numerical calculations omit any conceptual framing).  Hence the work meets âstrong understanding with partially-integrated analysisâ rather than the fully-integrated standard required for the top band.

annotations.comment: The title page includes the required IB elements (title and subject), demonstrating adherence to presentation requirements.
The inclusion of a word count meets IB guidelines, but ensure it falls within the specified range and consider flagging if it exceeds limits.
The contents page is clearly labelled and structured, aiding navigation through the reportâs sections.
The table of contents lacks page numbers for each heading; adding these will improve clarity and reader orientation.
The introduction effectively uses UK revenue figures to contextualise the impact of going cashless on Starbucksâ sales.
The forecasted growth figures from PayPal (2024) are cited without evaluation of their methodological robustness; consider discussing source credibility.
The key concept of âchangeâ is defined but could be better woven into each analytical point to deepen conceptual integration.
The assumption of 500 daily transactions lacks sourcing or justification; provide the data source or clarify the estimate.
The conversion of saved seconds directly into coffee production ignores practical constraints; consider potential bottlenecks in service.
Consider presenting equations using LaTeX notation (e.g., $$\text{Time saved/day}=500\times5$$) to enhance mathematical clarity.
The costâsavings table is well integrated and clearly displays transaction fees under each plan relevant to the analysis.
Link each demographic segment to projected cashless adoption behaviours to strengthen the connection to Starbucksâ change.
The customerâexclusion discussion cites a UK Parliament survey; critically assess sample size and representativeness before generalising.
The disadvantages section provides a balanced account of customer exclusion risks, showing thoughtful evaluation.
The inâtext reference to âappendix 2â is vague; clarify which row or fee the analysis uses to avoid reader confusion.
Expand the cost comparison by calculating per-transaction savings explicitly (e.g., savings per 100-unit transaction), then scale up.
The 90% cashless sales assumption is applied without validation; ground this figure in specific UK payment data or survey results.
The calculation of average transactions (Revenue Ã· Â£5) is presented step-by-step, enhancing transparency in the analysis.
Perform a sensitivity analysis showing how savings vary with different transaction values or cashless adoption rates.
The segmentation table effectively showcases demographic and psychographic criteria, supporting audience analysis.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: Segmentation data is sourced from a US context; adjust or comment on how UKâspecific demographics might differ.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: The conclusion reiterates key findings and directly answers the research question, showing consistency with the evidence presented.
Estimate potential revenue loss by quantifying excluded segments (e.g., % of 65+ customers Ã average spend).
Only Lewinâs Force Field Analysis is employed; consider supplementing with models like SWOT or costâbenefit analysis for robustness.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: The force-field analysis clearly distinguishes driving and resisting forces, highlighting key implementation considerations.
Provide the scoring criteria and rationale for each force (e.g., why speed scores 5/5) to enhance methodological transparency.
The conclusion omits specific changeâmanagement strategies to mitigate identified risks; adding these would strengthen the judgement.
Recommend a phased implementation plan or pilot testing to address potential customer and technical challenges.
